docs.title: Welcome to Yanis Kouidri's personal docs
docs.text: Work in progress

docs.title: Bun
docs.text:
Update deps interactively

bun update -i

Update bun itself

bun upgrade

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Before you run any command you hate to configure git once on your machine

git config --global user.name "Your name"

git config --global user.emal "Your email"

docs.title: Basic
docs.text:
Start a new project

git init

Clone a remote project (here an example with Win11Debloat)

git clone https://github.com/Raphire/Win11Debloat.git

docs.title: ACP cycle
docs.text:
ACP: Add, Commit, Push

To see what is modified, staged and your branch

docs.title: Add
docs.text: git add myfile.py

docs.title: Commit
docs.text: git commit -m "feat: add /user api endpoint"

docs.title: Push
docs.text:
git push -u origin main

Will push the local main branch over the remote main branch.

-u option allow saving the link between remote branch main and the local branch main. Thus, you will be able to only write git push.

docs.title: Branch
docs.text:
To see both remote and local branch

git branch -a

To see link between local and remote branch

git branch -vv

docs.title: Switch
docs.text:
To switch branch to dev

git switch dev

docs.title: Tags
docs.text:
While branch are an active line of development, tag are static pointer to a specific moment in your repo history.

Tags are immutable while branch are mutable.

When you are satisfied of your app, and you went to set a version run:

git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "First stable version with auth"

Then you have to push the tag.

git push origin v1.0.0

docs.title: Good tags workflow example
docs.text:
git add .

git commit -m "feat: add user logout"

git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "First version with auth"

git puhs origin main --follow-tags

This last command will push branch and tags.

docs.title: Rebase
docs.text: Rebasing allow you to reorganized pasted commits.

docs.title: Good rebase workflow example
docs.text:
Go to main

git switch main

Get last commit of main

git pull

Create a new branch for your feature

git switch -b feature/add-new-section

Code your feature and make several commits

git add .

git commit -m "wip: add something"

When you finish, get lasts commit of main that were created during development of your feature

git fetch origin

Apply your branch over the last commit of main, conflict may happend here

git rebase origin/main

Resolve conflicts if any then

git rebase --continue

Clean your commits

git rebase -i HEAD~10

Push the branch

git push --force-with-lease

docs.title: Delete empty replicas set
docs.text:
Delete all empty replicas set in the current namespace.

kubectl delete rs $(kubectl get rs -o jsonpath='{.items[?(@.status.replicas==0)].metadata.name}')

docs.title: Test a deployed app on local
docs.text:
When your app is deployed on Kubernetes thanks to a deployment and a service and you want to test it without expose it to Internet, you can use this:

Perform Kubernetes port forwarding:

kubectl port-forward svc/personal-website-v2 2040:80 -n personal-website-v2

Where:

  • 80: is the svc/personal-website-v2 port
  • 2040: is the port of the remote host (only accessible in localhost)

To access form your browser in your computer on a remote machine:

ssh ovh-vps -NL 7070:localhost:8080

Where:

  • ovh-vps: is the preconfigured ssh host where the Kubernetes app run
  • -N: "No command", ssh won't open a TTY on the remote host
  • -L: "Local port forwarding", to perform port forwarding
  • 7070: is the local port on your machine
  • 8080: is the port of the remote host that we open previously (only accessible in localhost so we tunneled it thanks to ssh)

With this solution, no port will be open the Kubernetes server.

docs.title: Change hostname
docs.text:
Change permanently with

sudo hostnamectl set-hostname linuxconfig

Check

hostnamectl

Reboot is required to show it in the prompt

docs.title: Add a new message
docs.text:
sudo vi /etc/update-motd.d/99-custom

Write something like that:

#!/bin/bash
echo """
SOME TEXT or ASCII Art
"""

Change permissions

sudo chmod 755 /etc/update-motd.d/99-custom

Logout login to check

docs.title: APT packet research
docs.text: When you want to execute a command, but you don't remind the name of the packet who contain this command you can try:

docs.title: Simply run the command
docs.text:
On Ubuntu, it will help you with the package to install:

$ traceroute
Command 'traceroute' not found, but can be installed with:
sudo apt install inetutils-traceroute  # version 2:2.6-1ubuntu3, or
sudo apt install traceroute            # version 1:2.1.6-1

docs.title: Search using apt-file
docs.text:
Install apt-file

sudo apt install apt-file

Update list

sudo apt-file update

If you know exactly the path you can try: Example for the dig command

apt-file find /usr/bin/dig -F

-F will search for this exact pattern

else you can try:

apt-file find '/dig$' -x

That will search all packets that contain a script named dig -x specify that pattern is a regex.

Example output:

apt-file find -x /dig$
    apparmor: /etc/apparmor.d/dig
    bind9-dnsutils: /usr/bin/dig
    epic4: /usr/share/epic4/script/dig
    epic4-help: /usr/share/epic4/help/8_Scripts/dig

Here we can guess that to correct one is bind9-dnsutils

docs.title: Journalctl
docs.text:
A CLI tool to manage logs.

To see all logs

journalctl

To see only 20 last logs

journalctl -n 20

To follow logs output

journalctl -f

To see kernel logs only

journalctl -k

To see specific process logs (example for ssh service)

journalctl -u ssh.service

To filter by time:

journalctl --since "2023-10-27 10:00:00" --until "2023-10-27 11:00:00"

To filter by log level:

journalctl -p 1 -b
  • -b: To see error only until last reboot
  • -p 1: To see log level 1 and above

There are 8 log levels, from the most critical to the most informative

Log level Name 0 emerg 1 alert 2 crit 3 err 4 warning 5 notice 6 info 7 debug

docs.title: tldr
docs.text:
Get useful command usage example

Install

sudo snap install tldr

Get tar usage example:

tldr tar

docs.title: sudoedit
docs.text: To edit a root file use sudoedit instead of sudu vi. It allows you to still have your user .vimrc config.

docs.title: Files and folders size
docs.text:
Get size of a folder

du -sh a_folder/

Get files and folders size with a TUI

Install

sudo apt install ncdu

Use (press q to quit)

ncdu .

docs.title: Install
docs.text: sudo apt install nmap

docs.title: Scan
docs.text:
Scan all up host in a network

nmap -sn -n 192.168.1.0/24
  • -sn: No post scan, it only checks if host is up
  • -n: No DNS name resolution

Scan a specific range of port

nmap -p 80-1024 192.168.1.150

Scan specific ports

nmap -p 22,80,443 192.168.1.150

Detect host OS

nmap -A 192.168.1.150

docs.title: SS
docs.text: ss stands for Socket Statistic. It is the up-to-date command to investigate on socket on a Linux machine. It replaces netstat. ss can be installed with sudo apt install iproute2.

docs.title: Common usage
docs.text:
To list all TCP and UDP sockets listening on the machine and their associated process:

sudo ss -tunlp
  • -t: TCP
  • -u: UDP
  • -n: No name resolution (80 instead of http)
  • -l: Listening sockets only (no established connection)
  • -p: Display with process is listening on that port (require sudo)

docs.title: nmcli
docs.text: nmcli is the tool of NetworkManager to manage networking on a client Linux machine. On server, it's Netplan that is used to manage networking. While ip command is used to debug or to set temporary interface, nmcli allow creating persistent interfaces.

docs.title: Wi-Fi
docs.text:
Check Wi-Fi access points available

nmcli dev wifi

docs.title: General
docs.text:
Show interfaces (virtual and physical) 

nmcli device

Show connections 

nmcli con show

Up or down a connection 

sudo nmcli con up "MY-CON"

docs.title: Scanned PDF
docs.text:
To reduce PDF size of scanned papers, add OCR and realign pages use ocrmypdf

Reduce size, clean and realign pages, rotate-pages and add french OCR :

ocrmypdf ocrmypdf --clean --deskew --optimize 3 --jbig2-lossy --rotate-pages -l fra --jpeg-quality 10 input.pdf output.pdf

You can play with --jpeg-quality to select a trade-off between quality and size from 1 (lowest size) to 100 (better quality)

You have to install tesseract-ocr-fra

sudo apt install tesseract-ocr-fra

docs.title: Automatically crop pictures from a scan
Install
docs.text:
sudo apt install imagemagick bc

Download multicrop

docs.title: Run
docs.text:
./multicrop -u 1 scan_pics.jpg extracted_pic.jpg

It will generate extracted_pic-000.jpg extracted_pic-001.jpg … depending on number of pictures found in scan_pics.jpg.

To run it on all scan in the current folder:

for f in *.jpg; do
    ./multicrop -u 1 "$f" "output/extracted_$f"
done

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
sudo apt install imagemagick

Be careful

magick must be >= 7.0.0, so Ubuntu must be at least 25.04

docs.title: Run
docs.text:
For one picture

magick picture.HEIC picture.jpg

For all HEIC pictures in the current folder

for f in *.HEIC; do magick $f ${f%.HEIC}.jpg; done

docs.title: General
docs.text:
Get the model:

cat /proc/device-tree/model

Get more info: (works only on Raspberry Pi OS)

pinout

docs.title: Toggle the first letter in a capital letter
docs.text: sed 's/\(^.\)/\u\1/g' file.txt

docs.title: Uninstall programs
docs.text: Download Bulk Crap Uninstaller, it's an open source program to properly uninstall programs.

docs.title: Manage startup program
docs.text: To precisely select which program will run automatically at login, download : Sysinternals Suite and run autoruns64

docs.title: Disable telemetry and unwanted feature
docs.text: Download and run O&O ShutUp10++
